content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

September 20, 2002

Harry A. Knapp, Mayor
City of South Pasadena
1414 Mission Street
South Pasadena, CA 91030

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-02-240

Dear Mayor Knapp:

This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of City of South Pasadena Planning Commissioner Virginia Paca regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTION

May Ms. Paca make presentations on behalf of her clients before the City of South Pasadena's Design Review Board?

CONCLUSION

Ms. Paca may make presentations before the design review board provided that she does not purport to be a member of the planning commission. Additionally, Ms. Paca may respond to necessary contact with agency staff concerning the processing or evaluation of drawings.

_________________________
¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-02-240
Page No. 2

FACTS

The City of South Pasadena has a planning commission organized under the
provisions of a general law city. Additionally, you have a design review board appointed
by the mayor with the consent of the council. Decisions of the design review board may
be appealed to the planning commission.

You recently appointed Ms. Virginia Paca, a sole proprietor architect, to the
planning commission. Ms. Paca on frequent occasion makes presentations to the design
review board. She has no staff or associate architects in her business. She is questioning
whether she has a conflict in presenting plans to the design review board. Ms. Paca has
committed to declining to represent clients during her term that require a conditional use
permit or a variance before the planning commission.²

ANALYSIS

No public official shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use
his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official
knows or has reason to know he or she has a financial interest. (Section 87100.) An
official is deemed to have a financial interest if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the
official or a member of his or her immediate family or on any source of income
aggregating $500 or more provided to the public official within 12 months prior to the
time when the decision is made. (Section 87103(c).)

As a member of the planning commission, Ms. Paca is a public official. (Section
82048.) Accordingly, she may not make, participate in making, or use her official
position to influence a governmental decision that would effect a client from whom she
has received $500 or more within the 12 months prior to presentation of the plans.

A public official makes a "governmental decision" when he or she votes on a
matter, obligates the agency to a course of action, or enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of the agency. (Regulation 18702.1.) An official "participates in
making a governmental decision" when he or she negotiates, advises or makes
recommendations to the decisionmaker. (Regulation 18702.2.) An official "attempts to
use his or her official position to influence a decision" if "the official contacts, or appears
before, or otherwise attempts to influence any member, officer, employee or consultant of
the agency." (Regulation 18702.3.)

-----------------------------------
² You also inquire as to whether a sole proprietor on the Cultural Heritage Commission may make
presentations to the design review board. Since we do not have an official of the Cultural Heritage
Commission seeking written advice, we decline to provide advice on this issue. (Regulation 18329, copy
enclosed.)

File No. A-02-240
Page No. 3

The issue you raise concerns "influencing a governmental decision." There are
two rules concerning whether a public official uses or attempts to use his or her official
position to influence a governmental decision. The first rule applies when a
governmental decision is within or before the public official's own agency, or an agency
appointed by or subject to the budgetary control of the public official's agency.
(Regulation 18702.3(a).) In that case,

"...the official is attempting to use his or her official position to
influence the decision if, for the purpose of influencing the decision,
the official contacts, or appears before, or otherwise attempts to
influence, any member, officer, employee or consultant of the
agency. Attempts to influence include, but are not limited to,
appearances or contacts by the official on behalf of a business entity,
client, or customer."

This prohibits Ms. Paca from making any presentations, or representing her
clients, before the planning commission, or any agency appointed by or subject to the
budgetary control of the planning commission. In your letter, you indicated that Ms.
Paca, a sole proprietor architect, does not intend to represent any clients before the
planning commission. You stated she would recuse herself and have the appeal presented
by someone else. Since she is a sole proprietor, there is no other member of her
architectural firm that could represent that client. In addition to recusing herself from
participating in decisions affecting a client of her firm, she also may not hire someone to
represent her client before the planning commission. Doing so would be an attempt to
influence the planning commission. (Freeman Advice Letter, No. I-90-664.)

The second rule applies when a governmental decision is within or before an
agency other than the public official's own agency, or an agency appointed by or subject
to the budgetary control of the public official's agency. Under this rule, an "official is
attempting to use his or her official position to influence the decision if, for the purpose
of influencing the decision, the official acts or purports to act on behalf of, or as the
representative of, his or her agency to any member, officer, employee or consultant of an
agency." (Regulation 18702.3(b).)

This provision applies when Ms. Paca makes a presentation before the design
review board or before any other agency not appointed by or subject to the budgetary
control of the planning commission. She would be permitted to make such presentations
on behalf of her clients provided she does not purport to be acting as member of the
planning commission.

There is an additional exception that may apply to Ms. Paca. Regulation
18702.4(b)(4) permits an official to prepare "drawings or submissions of an architectural,
engineering or similar nature to be used by a client in connection with a proceeding
before any agency. However, this provision applies only if the official has no other direct
oral or written contact with the agency with regard to the client's proceeding before the

File No. A-02-240
Page No. 4

agency except for necessary contact with agency staff concerning the processing or
evaluation of the drawings or submissions prepared by the official."3

This exception allows Ms. Paca to submit plans or drawings on behalf of a client
to the planning department and she may respond to "necessary contact with agency staff
concerning the processing or evaluation of the drawings." This limits her
communications to responding to staff questions relating to the evaluation of drawings or
relating to their movement through the approval process. (See Holbert Advice Letter,
No. I-90-080.)

Accordingly, Ms. Paca may make presentations before the design review board,
and any other agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary control of the planning
commission, provided she does not purport to be acting as a member of the planning
commission. Additionally, she may respond to necessary contact with agency staff
concerning the processing or evaluation of drawings, within the limitations described
above.

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

[signature]

By: Jeanette E. Turvill
Political Reform Consultant
Technical Assistance Division

JET:jg
I:\AdviceLtrs\02-240

3 Another exception applies only to members of a design review board. This exception provides
that an official may appear before a "design or architectural review committee or similar body of which he
or she is a member to present drawings or submissions of an architectural, engineering or similar nature"
provided specific criteria are met. (Regulation 18702.4(b)(5).) Since Ms. Paca is a member of the planning
commission, not the design review board, a discussion of this exception is irrelevant.
